Well, I can tell you many years ago I had a client go to prison and he was in prison for five years. And in the Ohio State Penal System, nobody can visit an inmate for the first six months unless they are either family or they're clergy. I wasn't family and I wanted to visit. So I got me one of them $100 cards from the Universal Life Church and became a minister. And by the way, it's credentials for life so I'm legally able to do weddings and funerals. I suggested to Carla that we say $50 and I do ours and I just jump back and forth to the two positions but she wouldn't buy it. I thought it would have been funny. She turns out she has a limited sense of humor about this whole wedding thing. Yeah, somebody just said they all do. So I get my Universal Life Church thing that I bought. Now remember, this is a while ago. And one of the things in the little manual you get it says is ministers routinely get upgraded to first class for free. Airlines upgrade ministers if they have an empty seat. How about that? That's an interesting piece of information. Since I'm hauling butt back and forth from Phoenix to Ohio like every week to go visit this guy and I'm broke, this is a real useful piece of information here if this is real. Also, the first time I try it, I get like nowhere. Then I give a little thought to this. Black suit, black collar, carry Bible. Boom! Upgraded. Same question, same card. The difference is the costume and the assumptions made based upon it. Flew first class for free, back and forth all year long. I think I got turned down once. All I had to do is wear a black suit, carry a Bible. Got it out of the drawer in the hotel. Free. Not a story you want to tell a lot probably. Here's Corta's line. Another book you ought to read. It's old. Michael Corta's book, Power. Very good book. Corta points out that costumes and other symbols vary in their significance based upon circumstance, context and person being influenced. In certain cases, the costumes write no matter what. So Wagner's movie star uniform is his movie star uniform pretty much no matter what. But often there's significant variance based on geography, industry, where they grew up, how old are they? Is there corporate culture? Is there any specific message you're trying to communicate? So you've got to think about the symbols you use in the context of who you are trying to influence, where in the country you or they are, what their background is, what they pay attention to, what is meaningful to them. Not just like a big general statement. Think about all of your symbols. Again this is old but it's still relevant from Corta. So this book goes back to when electric calculators were sort of new. And it may be hard to find, I don't know, but it's still a book well worth reading. But his point was if you've got an electric calculator and you know how to use it, it's a symbol you're a low level person, not a high level person. The highest level people don't have computers sitting on their desks, unless they're copy writers. They've got somebody that does that for them. I have several clients who have fake offices and real offices. They meet in the fake office, they work in the real office. The fake office is a set. I mean it's an office built to look like the office should look for the role that they play. And then they never take anybody into the real office where they work. It's not all that uncommon. So you want to think about what the symbols you wear, use, carry, what message that they convey. When I was young in this business, I carried a briefcase pretty much everywhere I went. Then I figured that out, stopped doing that, and started showing up empty handed. Because it turned out that the most powerful person in the room was the person who wasn't carrying anything. So once I figured that out, I don't want to be the guy schlepping a briefcase.
